source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic

extracts.extract: Faced with the provisional nature of the situation, I began to observe that, when drawing on public databases to write reports, journalists needed, for example, to expose the limitations of those records. They needed to explain that the numbers were not detailed or that there could be delays in entering information into public systems, which in turn would generate a temporary distortion in analyses.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Exposing data limitations and explaining record-entry delays to readers is a permanent transparency obligation

extracts.extract: In some cases, when certain statistics could not be used, data journalists would indicate, in the stories, why they decided to exclude one or another state from the analysis, or why such information should be observed with caution by the reader. They were, then, explaining their working method, informing what sources were consulted, how reliable those sources were, and how one or another result was reached.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Explaining methodological choices, source reliability, and exclusion decisions in published stories is timeless transparency practice

extracts.extract: Transparency in journalism carries the meaning of openness, of strategically managed visibility and of reduction of information asymmetry, since the journalist has first-hand access to data that the audience does not possess; thus, authors such as Kovach and Rosenstiel advocate that ways be found for journalism to compensate for this aspect, by revealing information about sources and reporting methods.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency as reduction of information asymmetry by revealing sources and methods is a foundational journalism value

extracts.extract: In Data-Driven Journalism, this includes, for example, providing hyperlinks to original databases and, when a programming language is employed, the source code that drove the collection, analysis, and results.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Linking to databases and source code endures but now extends to sharing LLM prompts and AI-assisted analysis pipelines

extracts.extract: Volunteers work with a shared methodology to follow the same data collection process; moreover, there is a constant information verification process — including a kind of independent double-check before updating the shared system with new data — in order to avoid errors. All methodology and source code are available on GitHub.
extracts.chapter: Public transparency and open data on Covid-19
extracts.relevance_rationale: Shared methodology, independent double-checking, and open-source code for verification are permanent best practices

extracts.extract: One of the first and most important lessons I drew from my experience with open data and the Covid-19 pandemic was precisely the precariousness in the collection and opening of information. Journalists were required to look skeptically at the statistics, understanding their contexts of production and availability.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Looking skeptically at statistics and understanding contexts of data production is a permanent verification stance

extracts.extract: Data-Driven Journalism is defined by Träsel as the application of computing and social science knowledge in the collection, processing, interpretation, and presentation of data, with the aim of expanding journalism's function as a defender of the public interest. I reiterate the definition that it is a set of practices and knowledge whose origins are associated with scientific knowledge, through truth and methodological objectivity, capable of offering high degrees of transparency and collaboration, producing enduring content.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Defining DDJ through scientific method, transparency, collaboration, and public interest is timeless

extracts.extract: The expression raw data is paradoxical. As the authors remind us, data do not exist by themselves in the world, but need to be collected, compiled, generated, and processed. Rosenberg explains that data derives from the verb dare, or to give, and is thus related to the production of an argument — that is, it presupposes an interpretive layer. Facts are ontological, evidence is epistemological, data is rhetorical.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The insight that raw data is paradoxical and data is rhetorical, not ontological, is a permanent epistemological truth

extracts.extract: Data-Driven Journalism has as one of its differentiators its proximity to the scientific method. Therefore it bets, for example, on the use of hypotheses in place of the conventional construction of a story pitch. This hypothesis will serve as the starting point for the investigation, which has its own method.
extracts.chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hypothesis-driven investigation as DDJ differentiator from conventional pitching is a permanent methodological principle

extracts.extract: Meyer suggests the following stages for journalism to approach science: collection — data gathering through scientific method; storage — comprising the organization and forms of data storage; retrieval — presupposes consulting data made available by third parties and offering ways of consultation; analysis — combining statistical tests and other ways of investigating patterns in relation to a topic; reduction — selection of what is maintained in the analysis and what will not be addressed; communication — data presentation, which in DDJ usually occurs through the use of charts.
extracts.chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Meyer six-stage scientific journalism workflow is a foundational reference model that persists conceptually

extracts.extract: The Escola de Dados developed its own methodology — the so-called Data Pipeline — to organize the stages that form a method: ask, find, obtain, verify, clean, analyze, and present. The main difference in relation to Meyer's proposal concerns the inclusion of a question as the starting point for obtaining data related to the topic to be addressed.
extracts.chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Escola de Dados pipeline endures conceptually but needs updating to include LLM-assisted steps in ask-find-obtain-verify-clean-analyze-present

extracts.extract: The Deutsche Welle Data-Driven Journalism team presents its own method for finding stories in databases. In general terms, the procedures involve: defining a main question or hypothesis, finding the data, verifying the source, understanding the data, cleaning the data, analyzing the data, analyzing the obtained results, visualizing the data, validating the results, and, finally, supplementing this work with reporting techniques.
extracts.chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: DW pipeline endures as process model but verification and analysis steps now incorporate LLM-assisted validation

extracts.extract: The proposals presented here are still insufficient with regard to transparency and data openness, essential to the scientific process on which DDJ is based. The proposals are also limited in terms of checking information, whose numbers are usually interpreted literally by journalists when presented by official sources, but not always exhaustively verified.
extracts.chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Critique that existing pipelines lack sufficient transparency and verification steps remains valid and even more relevant with AI

extracts.extract: Transparency in journalism corresponds, broadly speaking, to the process of opening up information from the journalistic organization and the processes it develops. At the organizational level, it presupposes the disclosure of funding sources and error-correction policies; at the operational level, it explains, for example, how a news piece was produced, why the topic matters, what sources were consulted, what the results of the analysis are, and the existence of any limitations.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency at both organizational and operational levels as journalism ethics principle is permanent

extracts.extract: The journalist needs to be a filter, as well as a transmitter; an organizer and interpreter, as well as one who gathers and delivers facts. The competency of writing well would no longer be sufficient.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Journalist as filter and interpreter endures but LLMs now assist with filtering and organizing, changing the required competency mix

extracts.extract: The sources and the method used must be transparent so that other journalists or experts can repeat the same procedure under the same conditions. They must also arrive at the same result.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparent methods enabling reproducibility by other journalists is a foundational scientific journalism principle

extracts.extract: Rigor is going to the bottom of the materially achievable investigations, in the time given for this research. It is the collection of all confirmed available facts. It is everything that opposes falsification, distortion, and lying.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Rigor as exhaustive investigation opposing falsification and distortion is a timeless journalistic value

extracts.extract: The emergence of practices like DDJ is closer to the promise of giving journalism a scientific character. They warn, however, that the authority lies not in simply showing the numbers and winning over audiences through their precision, but in the possibility of numbers contributing to the creation of a more rigorous discipline of verification. Data, therefore, demand greater methodological transparency.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data demanding greater methodological transparency to support verification discipline is permanently true

extracts.extract: Journalists place excessive trust in statistics provided by supposedly apolitical official sources, such as the United Nations and smaller non-governmental organizations. He concludes that journalists hide behind their sources, rarely verifying or questioning the numbers presented. The uncritical use of evidence of evidence allows journalists to protect themselves from criticism if they use incorrect statistics.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Critique of uncritical trust in official statistics and hiding behind sources remains deeply relevant

extracts.extract: Philip Meyer stated that readers deserve not just the facts, but the truth of the facts: to guarantee the reliability of information, journalists need to use belt and suspenders, making an analogy to the need for double-checking.
extracts.chapter: Transparency as the opening of method and editorial decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Double-checking as belt-and-suspenders reliability practice is a permanent verification principle

extracts.extract: The best journalism describes its methods with such clarity and precision that the reader is convinced that they could follow the same procedures and reach the same conclusions.
extracts.chapter: Transparency practices in DDJ newsrooms (Brazil and Germany)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Methods described with enough clarity for replication is the gold standard for transparent journalism, unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: In the case of DDJ, access to the original databases would allow the audience and other reporters to conduct their own analyses and judge the validity of the conclusions derived from a given journalistic piece.
extracts.chapter: Transparency practices in DDJ newsrooms (Brazil and Germany)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Public access to original databases enabling independent analysis and validation is a permanent transparency principle

extracts.extract: The Markup Method, according to editor Julia Angwin (2021), consists of the detailed publication of the methodology used in the reports. There are three main steps toward the scientific method: the elaboration of original data, peer review by specialists, and the communication of the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The Markup Method of publishing methodology with peer review steps is a model that endures and gains importance with AI

extracts.extract: For transparency to sustain itself as an operator of an ethical practice and a discursive resource of legitimation, it must, strictly speaking, be an element that resonates both at the organizational level and at the operational level — in this case, it must also be reflected in the product.
extracts.chapter: Transparency and its levels: organizational and operational
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency as ethical practice must resonate at organizational and operational levels -- a permanent governance principle

extracts.extract: The methodology employed in a Data-Driven Journalism report typically appears at the end of the text, in the form of a methodological note. This is a section that seeks to translate a series of procedures carried out by reporters to ensure clarity regarding the scope of data selection and collection, as well as to justify choices such as the adoption of certain sources and procedures.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Methodological notes explaining scope, sources, and procedural choices are a permanent DDJ transparency practice

extracts.extract: Traces of reporting refer to journalistic records that normally remain behind the scenes and are not exposed in published texts, but that help readers gain an overview of the difficulties involved in the reporting process. They differ from methodology in that they are more specific and subtle; they indicate, for example, how many times the journalist called a given source or contacted a press office, how many databases had to be searched to find a particular topic or analytical value, as well as errors found in the original documents, among other problems.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Traces of reporting showing behind-the-scenes difficulties help readers understand the full investigation context

extracts.extract: In addition to the possibility of describing the working method only in narrative form, one of the distinguishing features of DDJ is precisely organizing its methods in code format and, in this way, enabling the reproducibility and verification of information. Thus, other journalists and newsrooms are able to verify the content produced, as in a kind of peer review, and eventually reuse the code.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Code-based reproducibility endures but now must also account for LLM prompts, model versions, and non-deterministic AI outputs

extracts.extract: The proposal depicted in Figure 32 presents a series of steps for journalistic work. It starts from a problem (1) and a hypothesis (2) for which information must be sought through research (3), and whose databases present a context (4) about how they were produced and where they are situated. After examining the feasibility of using the information found, the process moves to the stages of collection (5) and analysis of information (6), which require the elaboration of lines of open source code (7) for subsequent reproducibility.
extracts.chapter: Proposed Method with a Focus on Transparency and Reproducibility
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pipeline starting from problem and hypothesis endures but open-source code requirement now extends to LLM prompt logs

extracts.extract: The insertion of information into a spreadsheet or other type of document involves an initial human effort and is therefore subject to mistakes. Thus, data must be treated as any documentary source and, for this reason, must be evaluated in their totality. I argue that the context of data must be studied as soon as the journalist finds the evidence they intend to work with.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Treating data as documentary source requiring full evaluation of context and provenance is permanently essential

extracts.extract: While replicability presupposes the use of code for new analyses, or could even refer to a methodology explained narratively, reproducibility involves the execution of step by step, allowing for the complete verification of results and their validation, similar to the premises adopted in open science.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distinction between replicability and reproducibility endures but must now address non-deterministic LLM outputs

extracts.extract: When spreadsheet software is used for data analysis, formulas, filters, and pivot tables are generally employed — processes that tend to remain hidden in the results. When the analytical stages are structured in a programming language, the tools allow the step-by-step to be commented and documented, making the procedures clear and accessible so that they can be replicated or reproduced later.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet analysis hides steps while code documents them -- true but LLMs now generate code, adding a new transparency layer to manage

extracts.extract: Data collection must be related to the problem and the hypothesis proposed at the beginning of the method, and therefore may involve combining different sources. Data collection is not always simple and direct — in some government portals, for example, data are made available in closed formats (such as .pdfs, which make it difficult to extract information). In these cases, we say that the data are available, but not accessible.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data collection from government portals with closed formats endures but LLMs can now assist with PDF extraction and format conversion

extracts.extract: The formulation of a hypothesis is central to scientific investigation. It functions as a kind of guide for the researcher, directing focus toward specific questions to be answered, and for the reader, who has a kind of plan and set of premises for understanding the results presented.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hypothesis formulation as central to scientific investigation guiding researcher and reader is a permanent methodological principle

extracts.extract: Especially at the beginning of the pandemic, the availability and organization of data related to Covid-19 presented gaps and problems, such as underreporting of cases. In this way, data journalists needed to seek alternatives. Upon finding databases that presented numbers about the pandemic, a kind of explanation about the uncertainties and limitations of the databases needed to accompany the reports.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Communicating uncertainty, limitations, and underreporting in data-driven stories is a permanent transparency obligation

extracts.extract: Whether public or developed within a journalistic organization, a dataset must be accompanied by comprehensive documentation so that the audience is able to understand how the information was collected and how it is presented. The sources, in turn, present their own context of production and the limitations inherent to the process.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Comprehensive dataset documentation explaining collection context and limitations is a permanent best practice

extracts.extract: Data analysis requires multiple precautions. It is first necessary to verify whether the figures form part of a dataset that exhibits a normal distribution. Numbers that do not belong to this orderly distribution are called outliers and, although journalism has a tradition of reporting singular cases, a single absolute or isolated number is rarely able to explain a phenomenon.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Verifying normal distribution, checking for outliers, and not relying on single absolute numbers are permanent analytical principles
